finalProject.htmlTemplate:
✨ Mad Libs

Simon Says Edition

Fill in the blanks to generate your silly story.

Your story will appear here once you fill in the blanks and hit Generate.

content.heading: Put it all together — Mad Libs

blocks.text: You've learned variables, strings, template literals, prompt(), comparisons, and conditionals. Now you're going to use all of them at once to build a Mad Libs game. The program will ask the player for a few words, then drop them into a silly Simon Says story.

blocks.label: What you're building
blocks.text: A program that asks the player for 6 words using prompt(), stores them in variables, assembles them into a story using a template literal, and changes the ending based on one of the answers using an if/else.

challenges.title: Try it yourself
tasks: Write your own Mad Libs story from scratch.
Pick a theme — it could be about anything: a school day, a pizza disaster, an alien visit.
Use at least 4 prompt() calls, a template literal, and one if/else.
There are no checks here — just make something you're proud of.
challenges.hint: Start with the variables, then the prompts, then the template literal, then the if/else. Same four steps as before.

exercises.title: Step 1 — Declare your variables
tasks: Every piece of information in the story needs a home.
Declare one variable for each blank in the story.
Fill in the missing keywords and values.
exercises.hint: Use let for every variable. Strings start empty with ''. The number starts at 0.

lines.text: // The six blanks in our story
lines.explanation: Each variable here will hold one word from the player. We declare them all up front before asking for any input — this is good practice so every variable exists before it is used.
adjective will hold a descriptive word like 'clumsy' or 'sparkling'. It starts empty because we haven't asked the player yet.
robotName will hold the name the player gives the robot. It's a string so it starts as an empty string.
noun will hold any object — 'banana', 'spatula', 'calculator'. Anything goes in Mad Libs.
color will hold the color of the flashing square — 'neon green', 'electric blue', whatever the player picks.
exclamation is something the robot shouts — 'BEEP!', 'OH NO!', 'BANANA!'. The sillier the better.
squareCount is a number — how many squares light up at once. It starts at 0 and will be replaced by whatever the player types. We'll use this number in the if/else at the end.

blanks.instruction: Drag the keyword that creates a new variable.
blanks.explanation: let is the keyword that creates a new variable in JavaScript. const would work too but we use let here because the values will change when we assign the prompt() results.

blanks.instruction: adjective will hold a string. Drag the starting value for an empty string.
blanks.explanation: An empty string '' is the right starting value for a text variable. It means 'nothing yet' — we'll fill it in the next step when we call prompt().

blanks.instruction: Same keyword as line 2 — drag it again.
blanks.explanation: Every variable declaration starts with let. The pattern is always the same: let, name, =, value.

blanks.instruction: Type the keyword that creates a new variable.
blanks.explanation: You've dragged it twice — now type it. let is the keyword for declaring a new variable in JavaScript.

blanks.instruction: squareCount is a number, not a string. Drag the correct starting value for a number variable.
blanks.explanation: Numbers don't use quotes. 0 is the right starting value — no quotes, just the digit. If you wrote '0' it would be a string and the math later would break.

exercises.title: Step 2 — Ask the player
tasks: Now fill each variable with a real answer from the player.
Use prompt() for the text answers.
Wrap squareCount in Number() so you can use it in math later.
exercises.hint: prompt() always returns a string. Number(prompt('...')) converts it to a number straight away.

lines.text: // Ask the player for each word
lines.explanation: We call prompt() once for each variable. Each call pauses the program, shows a pop-up, and stores whatever the player types back into the variable.
prompt() returns a string — whatever the player typed. We store it straight into adjective. No let this time because adjective already exists from Step 1.

lines.text: robotName   = prompt('Give the robot a name:');
lines.explanation: Same pattern. prompt() asks the question, the player types an answer, and the string comes back into robotName.

lines.text: noun        = prompt('Enter a random noun (e.g. banana, spatula):');
lines.explanation: Any noun works here. The sillier the better — that's the whole point of Mad Libs.
This will be the color of the flashing square in our story. 'Neon pink' or 'electric avocado' are both valid answers.

lines.text: exclamation = prompt('What does the robot shout? (e.g. BEEP, OH NO):');
lines.explanation: exclamation gets whatever the player shouts. All caps optional but encouraged.

lines.text: squareCount = <<bk_1_1>>(<<bk_1_2>>('How many squares lit up? Enter a number:'));
lines.explanation: squareCount needs to be a number so we can use it in an if/else later. We wrap prompt() in Number() to convert the string the player typed into an actual number.

blanks.instruction: Drag the function that shows a pop-up and asks the player to type something.
blanks.explanation: prompt() is the function that pauses the program, shows a message in a pop-up box, and returns whatever the player typed as a string.

blanks.instruction: squareCount needs to be a number for the if/else in Step 4. Drag the function that converts a string to a number.
blanks.explanation: Number() takes a string like '5' and converts it to the actual number 5. Without this, squareCount would be the string '5' and the comparison squareCount > 3 might not work as expected.

blanks.instruction: Drag prompt again — it goes inside Number() as the thing being converted.
blanks.explanation: Number(prompt('...')) is a common pattern: ask the player, get a string back, convert it to a number in one step. The prompt() call happens first, then Number() wraps the result.

exercises.title: Step 3 — Build the story
tasks: Now drop all six variables into the story using a template literal.
A template literal uses backticks (`) and ${} to slot variables in.
Fill in the missing variable names inside the ${} gaps.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes inside ${}. No quotes around it — it's a variable, not a string.

lines.text: // Build the story using a template literal
lines.explanation: A template literal is a string wrapped in backticks (`) instead of quotes. Inside it, ${} is a gap where a variable gets dropped in. JavaScript replaces each ${variableName} with the actual value stored in that variable.

lines.text: let story = `One day, a ${<<bk_2_0>>} robot named ${<<bk_2_1>>} decided to play Simon Says.`,
lines.explanation: The template literal starts here. ${adjective} will be replaced by whatever the player typed for their adjective. ${robotName} will be replaced by the robot's name.

lines.text: `The robot pressed a ${<<bk_2_2>>} to start the game.`,
lines.explanation: story is an array of sentence strings. Each line is one sentence. ${noun} drops the player's noun right into the sentence.

lines.text: `The first square lit up ${<<bk_2_3>>} and the robot shouted '${<<bk_2_4>>}!'`,
lines.explanation: Two variables in one sentence — ${color} and ${exclamation}. The order inside the template literal is what decides where each word appears in the story.

lines.text: `Then ${<<bk_2_5>>} more squares lit up all at once.`;
lines.explanation: squareCount is a number but template literals handle that automatically — it gets converted to a string when dropped into the sentence. No Number() or String() needed here.
story is an array, so we loop through it and log each sentence one at a time. This way each sentence appears on its own line in the console.
forEach runs a function once for each item in the array. Each time it runs, 'sentence' holds one sentence from the story.

blanks.instruction: Drag the variable that holds the player's adjective into this gap.
blanks.explanation: Inside ${} you write the variable name — no quotes. JavaScript looks up the value stored in that variable and drops it into the string.

blanks.instruction: Drag the variable that holds the robot's name.
blanks.explanation: robotName holds the string the player typed for the robot's name. It goes straight into the sentence here.

blanks.instruction: Drag the variable that holds the player's noun.
blanks.explanation: noun holds whatever random object the player typed. Could be 'banana'. Could be 'fire extinguisher'. The template literal doesn't care — it just drops it in.

blanks.instruction: Drag the variable that holds the player's color.
blanks.explanation: color holds the color string. It gets dropped right into the sentence between 'lit up' and 'and the robot'.

blanks.instruction: Drag the variable that holds what the robot shouts.
blanks.explanation: exclamation holds the player's shout. The quotes around ${exclamation} in the sentence are part of the story text — they will appear literally in the output.

blanks.instruction: Type the variable that holds the number of squares. You've dragged the others — now type this one.
blanks.explanation: squareCount is a number but template literals convert it to a string automatically. You don't need to wrap it in String() — ${squareCount} just works.

exercises.title: Step 4 — The twist ending
tasks: Add a twist ending that changes based on squareCount.
If the player said more than 3 squares lit up — print a dramatic ending.
Fill in the condition and the two log messages.
exercises.hint: The condition goes inside the if(). Use > to check if squareCount is greater than 3.

checks.label: squareCount is compared to 3 in the condition

lines.text: // The twist ending — changes based on squareCount
lines.explanation: An if/else lets the story end differently depending on what the player answered. This is how games respond to player choices — the same code runs, but a different path is taken each time.
The condition goes inside the parentheses. If squareCount is greater than 3, the dramatic ending runs. If not, the else block runs instead.
This line runs when squareCount is greater than 3. We use a template literal so robotName gets dropped into the dramatic ending message.
The else block runs when the condition was false — when squareCount is 3 or fewer.

lines.text: console.log('Not bad for a first try. The robot will do better next time.');
lines.explanation: This is the calmer ending. It runs when the player said 3 or fewer squares lit up — a smaller challenge, a smaller reaction.

blanks.instruction: Drag the condition that checks whether squareCount is more than 3.
blanks.explanation: squareCount > 3 is true when the player typed a number greater than 3. The > operator means 'greater than'. If the player typed 4 or more, this condition is true and the dramatic ending runs.

blanks.answer: `Whoa — ${squareCount} squares?! ${robotName} was completely overwhelmed. Game over!`
blanks.instruction: Type the dramatic ending. Use a template literal that includes both robotName and squareCount in the message.
blanks.explanation: A template literal lets you build a personalised message using the player's own inputs. The backticks wrap the whole string, and ${} slots in the variables. The message will be different every time based on what the player typed.
